Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, taxes. If we agree on the facts, the rest is easier.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the basics: roughly what’s coming in each year for the household—before taxes—so I’m not guessing?
Client 2: Household gross is 70700 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I split it correctly—Susan, what’s your piece of that, and Thoma, what’s yours?
Client 1: Mine is 41450 a year. I’m retired—mostly Social Security and a little bit of other income.

Client 1: That’s basically it. It’s not a bunch of different streams.
Advisor: Great. Let’s flip to spending. About what do you two spend per month all-in—housing, food, insurance, the usual stuff?
Client 1: We try to keep it pretty steady—3000 a month.
Advisor: Okay. And within that, do you have any debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards—anything that’s a required monthly payment?
Client 2: No. Monthly debt payments are 0. We don’t have loans outstanding.

Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, taxes. If we agree on the facts, the rest is easier.

Client 1: Mine is 41450 a year. I’m retired—mostly Social Security and a little bit of other income.

Client 2: No. Monthly debt payments are 0. We don’t have loans outstanding.
Advisor: That’s helpful. Now I want to understand what you’ve got set aside—investments and accounts you can draw from. About how much in investable assets total?
Client 2: Total investable assets are 362850.
Advisor: Okay. Can we walk through the main buckets so I understand where it sits and whose name it’s in?
Client 2: Sure. We have a joint taxable account on an advisor platform—value is 185900.

Client 1: Yes, I have private markets held at a bank—28450.
Advisor: Okay. And then the home—tell me about the house value and whether there’s any loan against it.
Client 1: The primary residence is 333,000, and there’s no mortgage.
Advisor: Got it. I heard you say it’s held at a brokerage—just meaning that’s where you track it, right?
Client 2: Right, it’s listed through the brokerage portal, but it’s our home—value 333,000.

Advisor: Helpful. Next one’s a little administrative: what state do you consider your primary residence for—where you file and vote, that kind of thing?
Client 2: It’s listed as Other.
Advisor: Other—okay. And then risk—when markets get choppy, how would you describe your comfort level? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits you best as a household?
Client 1: I’d say moderate. I get nervous if it swings too much, but we’re not hiding in cash either.
Advisor: That makes sense. Thoma, same page for you—moderate overall, with some guardrails?

Client 1: That’s exactly what I want—no surprises.
Client 2: Same. Just keep it straightforward.
Advisor: Okay—thank you both. Before we wrap today, I want to grab a couple of household basics that help me frame recommendations. Susan, Thoma—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And do you have any dependents—kids or anyone financially dependent on you right now?

Client 2: Household gross is 70700 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I split it correctly—Susan, what’s your piece of that, and Thoma, what’s yours?
Client 1: Mine is 41450 a year. I’m retired—mostly Social Security and a little bit of other income.
Client 2: And mine is 29250 a year. Also retired.
Advisor: Okay. Now, Thoma, when you say income, what are the main sources—pension, investment income, that kind of thing?

Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, taxes. If we agree on the facts, the rest is easier.

Advisor: Hi Susan, hi Thoma—thanks for coming in. I’m glad we could get this first meeting on the calendar. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today feel useful for you two?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d like to feel like we have a plan that keeps the bills covered and doesn’t get scary if health stuff changes.
Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, taxes. If we agree on the facts, the rest is easier.
Advisor: Perfect. Let’s start with the basics: roughly what’s coming in each year for the household—before taxes—so I’m not guessing?
Client 2: Household gross is 70700 a year.

Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Okay. And on the tax side—when you think about your marginal bracket lately, what band have you been landing in?
Client 2: We’ve been in the 35% bracket.
Advisor: Got it. And last admin-ish one: how are you expecting to work with me—do you want this through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).

Advisor: Okay. And on the tax side—when you think about your marginal bracket lately, what band have you been landing in?
Client 2: We’ve been in the 35% bracket.
Advisor: Got it. And last admin-ish one: how are you expecting to work with me—do you want this through an RIA, a registered investment advisor relationship, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just writing that down.

Advisor: Hi Susan, hi Thoma—thanks for coming in. I’m glad we could get this first meeting on the calendar. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today feel useful for you two?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d like to feel like we have a plan that keeps the bills covered and doesn’t get scary if health stuff changes.
Client 2: And I want the numbers to line up—income, spending, taxes. If we agree on the facts, the rest is easier.

Advisor: Got it. And just so I split it correctly—Susan, what’s your piece of that, and Thoma, what’s yours?
Client 1: Mine is 41450 a year. I’m retired—mostly Social Security and a little bit of other income.
Client 2: And mine is 29250 a year. Also retired.
Advisor: Okay. Now, Thoma, when you say income, what are the main sources—pension, investment income, that kind of thing?
Client 2: For me it’s a pension and then interest and dividends. The pension is 59850 annually, paid monthly, and the interest and dividends are 10850 annually, also paid monthly.
